fernando mendoza.play_style: Polished pocket passer with prototype 6-5/225 frame. Heisman winner who led Indiana to 16-0 CFP championship; FBS-leading 41 passing TDs, 72% completion, 9.3 Y/A in Big Ten (on par with recent #1s Caleb Williams and Cam Ward).
strengths: Elite post-snap processing — reads defenses quickly, anticipates zone windows before they open
Ball placement and accuracy are his best trait — 93.3 PFF passing grade over the middle (#2 among draft-eligible QBs)
Prototype pocket-passer build and polished passing skill set that translates directly to the NFL
Dissects coverages, finds the open man, throws with anticipation
weaknesses: Almost no under-center experience (3% of snaps in 2025, mostly shotgun)
Indiana's RPO-heavy scheme limited the NFL-style throws he put on tape
Pocket presence / feel needs NFL-level refinement early

david bailey.play_style: Twitched-up speed rusher (6-3/250, 4.5 forty, 1.62 ten-split) — unanimous All-American and Big 12 DPOY with 14.5 sacks (led FBS) at Texas Tech. Elite first step, multiple pass-rush moves, but built more on speed than power.
strengths: Elite first step / get-off — among the best in the class; 4.5 forty with 1.62-second 10-yard split
Long arms (33 3/4") and 79 3/4" wingspan, elite closing burst
Quick, active hands — deep arsenal: swipe, rip, ghost, inside spin, long-arm
Pure speed-to-edge is his hallmark; quarterbacks are in trouble once he clears the corner
weaknesses: Run defense concerns — gets displaced at point of attack, anchor strength lacking
Slightly undersized for full-time NFL edge (came up from 238 to 250 lbs; may have maxed frame)
Lacks power in his rush — over-relies on speed; needs to develop power counters
Speed-to-power conversion weak when tackles get hands inside frame

jeremiyah love.play_style: Elite three-down workhorse with rare speed/explosiveness/contact balance combo. Jahmyr Gibbs comps. Ran 4.36 at combine at his weight. 2,882 rush yds / 36 TDs + 63 catches at Notre Dame. #3 overall pick (Cardinals) — first top-5 RB since 2018.
strengths: Legitimately rare speed, explosiveness, quickness, agility (4.36 forty)
Elite contact balance and vision
Zone-scheme natural — patience behind the line with burst to hit the hole
Receiver-like hands and ball skills out of the backfield — 63 rec / 594 yds in college
Three-down capable (run between tackles, catch, protect)
Take-it-to-the-house home-run ability
weaknesses: Pass protection still developing (though he contributes)
Minor refinement needed in pass-pro technique and inside running patience

carnell tate.play_style: Polished, technical 6-2/192 WR. Wins with precision, body control, and coverage recognition. Drafted #4 overall (Titans). Chris Olave / George Pickens comps. Route technician who saw favorable matchups opposite Jeremiah Smith at OSU.
strengths: Route-running technician — runs every branch on the tree, manipulates DBs
Elite ball tracking and hands — extends and plucks away from frame, very soft hands
Outstanding body control — 'The Great Adjuster'; twists/turns to finish contested catches
Climbs the ladder well; bouncy athlete with large catch radius
High football IQ — reads coverage, finds space vs zone, mistake-free
Willing blocker; high character
weaknesses: Production modest for a top-5 pick (51-875-9 in 11 games as junior)
Untested as a true WR1 — saw favorable coverage opposite Jeremiah Smith
Smaller than listed (measured 6-2/192 at combine vs 6-3/195 billed)
Not a burner — wins with technique more than pure speed
carnell tate.nfl_comp: Chris Olave / George Pickens (with better hands)

arvell reese.play_style: Converted LB turned edge — tall, long, twitched-up with explosive get-off. Drafted #5 overall by Giants. High-ceiling athlete with uncommon Front-7 versatility; can drop into zone coverage. Still raw as a pass rusher.
strengths: Explosive, twitched-up athlete with above-average length and sculpted frame
Stronger than frame suggests — converts speed to power vs tackles
Agility + explosiveness to blow past unprepared blockers
Rare zone-coverage fluency for an edge — pedal-to-drive, matches TEs/RBs in space
Converted from LB — Pro Bowl upside with high athletic ceiling
weaknesses: Short arms for edge (32.5") — concern at top-5 slot
Pass-rush plan/arsenal still developing — only rushed on ~25% of pass snaps
Weak lateral movement / short-area quickness — struggles in man vs TEs/RBs
Bad eyes in zone at times — covers grass; late reacting to play-action

mansoor delane.play_style: Scheme-independent press/zone corner. 4.38 speed erased long-speed concerns. LSU transfer with 0 TDs allowed and <27% completion on 200+ coverage snaps in 2025. High-floor, instinctive technician. #6 to Chiefs (trade-up).
strengths: Elite coverage production — zero TDs allowed, sub-27% completion, only 2 rec 20+ yds
4.38 forty — recovery burst to carry vertical routes
Scheme-versatile — thrived in VT zone + LSU man looks
Advanced technique and hasty click-and-close ability in zone
Triggers downhill in run support, wraps up despite lean frame
Instinctive ball-hawk with 'hammer mentality' at catch point
weaknesses: Below-average arm length (30") — disrupted less vs tall boundary WRs
Lean frame (190 lbs) — durability / bigger-slot / TE matchup questions
Eyes can get manipulated by pump fakes and double moves
Needs to clean up grabbiness in coverage (penalty risk)
Functional strength still developing for a 17-game season

sonny styles.play_style: 6-5/244 converted-safety LB with rare athletic profile (92nd%ile wingspan, 99th%ile vert, 98th%ile broad). Fred Warner comps. First-team All-American at Ohio State. #7 overall. Elite coverage LB with blitz upside still untapped.
strengths: Rare size/length/explosiveness combo for a LB — 6-5/244 with elite jumps
Excellent coverage awareness, positioning, and range on passing downs
Executes multiple coverage assignments comfortably (zone, man on TEs/RBs)
Team captain / green-dot wearer for CFP program — leadership/IQ
Blitz reps show real burst and power — untapped pass-rush upside
weaknesses: Tackle form — attempts get high, long frame creates leverage issues vs NFL RBs
Bites on play-action and boot fakes; vacates gap early
Play strength at POA vs climbing interior OL still developing
Not a ballhawk — only 1 career INT at OSU
Can over-rely on athleticism in space

jordyn tyson.play_style: Twitchy 6-2/200 matchup WR from Arizona State. Big 12 Offensive Newcomer of the Year with 75-1101-10 in 2024. Drafted #8 by Saints. Nuanced route runner; can line up anywhere. Injury history is the main red flag.
strengths: Smooth, nuanced route runner — manipulates DB leverage, uses tempo to create separation
Elite vs man coverage — 89.0 PFF receiving grade vs man in 2025, 4.63 YPRR vs man
Strong hands — 1.6% drop rate in 2025; tracks ball well; contested-catch capable
Versatile — can win at every level and play any WR spot
Physical run blocker for a WR; competitive demeanor
weaknesses: Serious injury history — torn ACL/MCL/PCL at Colorado (2022), broken collarbone (2024), hamstring (2025)
Lack of play strength for his size — doesn't dominate through contact as expected
Can struggle vs physical press coverage
Questions about top-end speed / ability to play faster
jordyn tyson.nfl_comp: Matchup X receiver with WR1 upside

spencer fano.play_style: Utah RT — unanimous All-American, Outland Trophy winner. Elite combine testing (93 athleticism / 85 production / 89 total — all best among OTs). #9 to Browns. Technically polished pass protector; frame questions on POA power.
strengths: Top athletic testing among OTs — elite feet, demeanor, and recovery balance
Dominant 2025 pass protection — 0 sacks allowed, 5 pressures / 5 hurries on 357 snaps
NFL-ready footwork and kick slide; rarely beaten cleanly
Unanimous All-American — consistent against SEC/Big 12 level competition
weaknesses: Core strength / pad level issues — gets overtaken late in reps as a drive blocker
Effort blocker in run game but can be walked back by elite power
NFL projection may be scheme/matchup dependent until he adds functional strength

francis mauigoa.play_style: Massive 6-5/329 Miami RT — widely considered the top OL prospect in the class. Power-based anchor who halts bull rush and generates displacement in the run game. #10 overall (Giants). Herniated-disk health flag.
strengths: Elite play strength and point-of-attack power — named ESPN's best anchor
Commanding bull-rush defender; generates displacement in run game
PFF 87.0 pass-blocking grade (7th among OTs); 83.6 overall
Densely built, NFL-ready frame — called the 'most NFL-ready OT' in class by Randy Cross
Versatile projection — can kick inside to guard if needed
weaknesses: Range / redirect ability closer to adequate — shorter corners when rushers gain width
Inside-shoulder exposure to counters vs quicker rushers
Not the quickest or most agile OT in the class
Herniated disk concern — may require surgery, could miss rookie-season time

caleb downs.play_style: Ohio State safety drafted #11 by Cowboys (trade-up). Elite football IQ and versatility — played all three secondary spots. Did not allow a TD in two seasons; cerebral, coverage-first prospect. Athletic profile more good than elite.
strengths: Elite football IQ — 'step ahead of QBs', cerebral processor
Zero TDs allowed over two seasons at OSU; sub-53.5 NFL QBR when targeted
Tackles with controlled aggression and textbook technique
Versatile — plays deep, slot, and box equally well
CB-level hip fluidity for a safety — mirrors route breaks in-phase
weaknesses: Not the biggest or fastest safety — athletic profile good, not elite
Some injury-history concerns raised privately
Play-speed on tape outstrips measured speed; depends on processing edge

kadyn proctor.play_style: Alabama LT — mountain (352 lbs) with rare size and natural power. Drafted #12 (Dolphins). Road-grader run blocker with heavy hands and strong anchor. Pass-pro technique inconsistent; possible guard convert long-term.
strengths: Rare size/power combo — arguably best physical traits in class
Elite anchor — power rushers go nowhere when he drops it
Road-grader in the run game; strong hands + thick base
Outstanding combine testing for his mass — 32.5" vert, 109" broad
weaknesses: Pass-protection is a roller coaster — some snaps great, others raw
Top-heavy play style, inconsistent technique — clean losses on island reps
Lack of ideal foot speed and arm length — potential guard projection
High ceiling but low floor early; Round 2 grade despite Round 1 capital
kadyn proctor.nfl_comp: Massive power LT with guard fallback

ty simpson.play_style: Alabama QB drafted #13 by Rams. Polished, repeatable mechanics — arguably more pro-ready than Mendoza mechanically. Only 15 career starts (sat behind Milroe). High-floor / low-ceiling projection; size concerns.
strengths: One of the best pre- and post-snap processors in class — works 1-2-3 reads
Tight, compact release with smooth, repeatable mechanics
Quality thrower on the move — excellent on bootlegs / rollouts
Controls defenders with his eyes; gets to checkdowns; pro-style concept mastery
Good lower-body strength — generates velocity and power
weaknesses: Limited starting experience (15 career starts); tape got worse as DCs adjusted
Anticipation from pocket needs work — late on out-breaking routes
Stares down predetermined reads — turnover risk
Under 6-1 and <215 lbs — historical size/durability concern
Gunslinger mentality — passes on checkdowns for downfield shots

olaivavega ioane.play_style: Penn State — top pure guard in the class. 6-4/320 power-based IOL. Drafted #14 by Ravens. Light-footed anchor who stonewalls bull rushers; limitations show up in space.
strengths: Top pure guard in class — halts bull rush with firm anchor
Light-footed for his size — mirrors athletic interior rushers
Stonewalls power DTs; rarely gives ground in pass protection
Allowed 0 hits / 0 sacks in 2025; patient, balanced footwork
Tone-setter in downhill or balanced run schemes
weaknesses: Average movement skills — limited winning in space
Struggles as a move blocker on reach blocks and second-level climbs
5.35 forty (42nd %ile) shows up vs LBs beating him to spot
Redirect ability vs quicker interior rushers grades average

rueben bain.play_style: Miami edge — 6-3/270 thickly built power rusher. Drafted #15 by Buccaneers. 20.5 career sacks. Violent hands, elite run defender, bull-rush base with a devastating move arsenal.
strengths: Bursts off the line — beats tackles to the punch with violent hands
Power-based edge — collapses pockets; jolts blockers back
Elite run defender — gap/scheme-disciplined, great ball location
Deep move arsenal — devastating swim, bull rush, counters
Unusual bend for his mass — maintains balance at extreme angles
Modern NFL DL — can line up anywhere on the front
weaknesses: Short arms (30 7/8") — major concern at high-impact NFL edge
Lacks some suddenness / redirect quickness
Leaves sacks on the field when initial move doesn't connect

kenyon sadiq.play_style: Oregon TE drafted #16 by Jets. Ran 4.39 at combine — fastest TE time in 14 years. Elite Y/F flex athlete with real blocking chops. Drop-rate concerns the main flaw.
strengths: Blazing 4.39 forty — best TE time in 14 Combines
Dangerous 3-level receiver with quick cuts and agility
One of most physical/versatile blockers in the class — great pad level
Elite body control and burst off snap
Tenacious competitor as a blocker; good technique
weaknesses: Drop-rate regression in 2025 (9.0%, 5th-highest among 50+ target TEs)
Inconsistent catch mechanics — fights routine catches at times
Slightly undersized for in-line dirty work
Height/length may limit TE1 usage in some schemes

blake miller.play_style: Clemson RT — 6-7/317, broke Clemson career-snaps record (3,778 snaps, 54 starts). Drafted #17 by Lions. Durable, experienced, safe prospect. Grit Index #1 among OTs.
strengths: Durable and consistent — 54 career starts, all at RT
Top-tier work ethic — Grit Index 9.67 (#1 among OTs)
Good athletic ability, arm length, quick proactive hands
PFF pass-blocking grade 81.6 (33rd of 632 qualified OTs in 2025)
Experienced, safe, alert starter-ready profile
weaknesses: Lacks high-end power — brings tenacity to finish but not dominance
Unproven versatility — played exclusively RT
Finisher more than road-grader

caleb banks.play_style: Florida DT drafted #18 by Vikings. 6-6/327 with explosive first step and elite length. Chris Jones comps. Foot injury limited him to 3 games in 2025; cleared for June.
strengths: 6-6/327 with elite length and explosive first step
Violent hands + nimble feet — Chris Jones comp from Bucky Brooks
Delivers real knockback power; good pad level for his height
Near-sub-5 forty (5.04) despite fresh-cleat irritation at Combine
Game-wrecker upside if it clicks
weaknesses: Motor burns hot and dies fast — disappears on second-wave rush reps
Won't produce cleanup sacks if he doesn't win initially
2025 foot injury (fractured 4th metatarsal) — only 3 games, March surgery
Raw — development path longer than production profile suggests

monroe freeling.play_style: Georgia LT — 6-7/315, 12 starts in 2025 (only year as full-time starter). Drafted #19 by Panthers. Plus-plus pass protector; run-game technique raw. Basketball-background athlete.
strengths: Plus-plus pass blocker — minimal pressure on ~750 snaps in SEC
Basketball-athletic for 6-7 frame — quick first step out of stance
Climbs to second level effortlessly; nimble for size
Reaches spot before defender arrives in pass set
weaknesses: Struggles in the run game — trouble sealing defenders on outside runs
Upright drive-block style — overextends and falls off blocks
Lacks starting experience — only 12 career starts as full-time starter
Readiness to start Year 1 questioned by scouts

makai lemon.play_style: USC WR — Biletnikoff winner. Drafted #20 by Eagles. Compact, competitive slot receiver with elite hands and YAC ability. Led top WRs in tackle-avoided rate, explosive-catch rate, and YPRR.
strengths: Biletnikoff winner — Best Receiver in college football
Elite hands, route feel, and run-after-catch ability
Led top WRs in tackle-avoided rate (26.6%), explosive-catch rate (28.7%), YPRR (3.22)
Very good contact balance; fights through tackles
Good play strength for size — holds up at catch point, sustains blocks
Toughness and versatility — chain-mover slot archetype
weaknesses: Pedestrian athletic profile — average explosiveness and long speed
Below-average size and catch radius; small hands
May need to add weight/strength to handle NFL DBs
Primarily slot projection — not a true X

max iheanachor.play_style: Arizona State OT — traits-heavy ascending prospect, former high school hooper, late-comer to football. Drafted #21. Ideal length + lean mass; technique still catching up to tools.
strengths: Ideal NFL size and length — elite lean mass
Explosive kick slide with natural knee bend
Excellent lateral mover — fits any run scheme
Strength anchors vs bull rush when set
Heavy hands when placed correctly
weaknesses: Late to punch — gives up his chest; punch placement too wide
Inconsistent anchor; lacks power in his punch
Speed rushers win the corner on him
Plays too high in pass pro — bendy rushers get under him
Not a road-grader in run game

akheem mesidor.play_style: Miami edge — 6-3/259 Canadian product drafted #22 by Chargers. Quick-twitch rusher with excellent hand usage, versatility to kick inside. 17.5 TFL / 12.5 sacks / 4 FF in 2025 — elite production.
strengths: Elite hand usage — wins around edge and across tackles' faces
Pass-rush production — 12.5 sacks / 17.5 TFL / 4 FF (only 2 FBS players with that stat line)
Good bend, closing burst, and power for his size
Versatile — can shade inside to DT and pressure the interior
High motor — keeps battling when initial move is thwarted
Impact vs both run and pass
weaknesses: Subpar strength vs anchored tackles — loses power-rush reps
Struggles to convert speed to power
Shoulders turn perpendicular when setting edge — run-D leverage concern
Not elite size for full-time NFL edge

malachi lawrence.play_style: UCF edge — 6-4/253, late riser after East-West Shrine + Combine. 4.52 forty. Drafted #23. Speed-and-bend specialist; small/light for run defense.
strengths: Rare lateral agility for size — dances in and out of crevices
4.52 forty with explosive jumps — closes on QBs fast
Deep pass-rush move arsenal — wins inside and outside
Nice first step + bend to flatten rush angle
20 career sacks on 28 TFLs — productive and takeaway-threat
weaknesses: Below-average size and strength — plays soft vs the run
Wide hand placement vs run game — can't extend/escape blocks
Struggles to anchor; misses tackles in space
Designated pass rusher projection — not every-down yet
Needs to add mass to handle NFL tackles

kc concepcion.play_style: Texas A&M WR drafted #24 by Browns. 5-11/196 YAC/slot weapon — led SEC in receiving TDs (9) and punt return yards (456). Shifty chess-piece; drop rate the main knock.
strengths: Elite YAC / elusiveness — go-the-distance threat on any touch
Versatile usage — bubble screens, jet sweeps, motion, handoffs
Very strong for size — welcomes contact; understands leverage
Sudden, deceptive route runner vs man and zone
Strong release package; quick lateral footwork through press
Finds voids vs zone; manipulates spot droppers
weaknesses: Focus drops — 10.3% drop rate in 2025
Hand mechanics need NFL-level cleanup; eyes leave ball early
Functional wingspan limits contested-catch vs longer NFL CBs
Undersized — primarily a slot projection
kc concepcion.nfl_comp: Shifty slot chess piece / return specialist

dillon thieneman.play_style: Oregon safety drafted #25 by Bears. One of the most athletic DBs in class — 4.3 speed, elite explosive testing. Ultra-instinctive, 8 career INTs. Size is the only real limitation.
strengths: Elite athleticism — 4.3 speed, elite vert/broad, great quickness/agility
Ultra-instinctive — 'felt like a 12th player'; proactive, always a step ahead
True centerfielder range — gets off hash; deep-post comfort
Versatile — plays deep, robber, and box safety
Exceptional communicator — diagnoses pre-snap like he's in the huddle
8 INTs in three safety seasons — real ball production
weaknesses: Size — doesn't have elite frame to match elite athleticism
Needs more functional mass to take on blocks
Over-aggressive vs play-action at times
Man-coverage projection more limited than zone/hole
Not elite laterally

keylan rutledge.play_style: Georgia Tech guard drafted #26 by Texans (trade-up). 6-3/316 mauler with mean streak. 9.53 RAS with elite agility testing. Broad-framed rag-doller who finishes blocks with bad intentions.
strengths: Broad-framed mauler with heavy hands and mean streak
9.53 RAS — elite combine agility testing
Well-built, mobile guard with good anchor strength
Rag-dolls defenders at POA; elite finisher
weaknesses: Shorter than ideal (6-3) for some schemes
Technique still catching up to physical tools
Trade-up capital may exceed pure evaluator grade

chris johnson.play_style: San Diego State CB drafted #27 by Dolphins. 6-0/193. Posted highest NFL Next Gen Stats athleticism score (87) among CBs at Combine. Versatile boundary/slot projection.
strengths: Elite athletic testing — 87 athleticism score (#1 CB at Combine)
1.54 10-yard split; 10-foot-6 broad jump
6-0/193 with 30 5/8" arms — adequate boundary size
Versatile — plays boundary or slot equally well
Disciplined, controlled in coverage — reacts without overcommitting
weaknesses: Non-Power-5 competition — small-school jump to NFL
Technical discipline strong but may need scheme stability early
Projects as Byron Murphy-type CB2 rather than CB1

caleb lomu.play_style: Utah LT drafted #28 by Patriots. 6-7/313 with 33 3/8" arms; 9.77 RAS (elite). Redshirt sophomore with only 24 career starts but elite pass-pro traits — 0 sacks allowed, 2.3% pressure rate on 359 snaps in 2025.
strengths: Elite pass-protection production — 0 sacks, 2.3% pressure rate on 359 snaps
9.77 RAS — elite testing: 4.99 forty, 32.5" vert, 9'5" broad, 25 bench
Ideal size/length — 6-7 1/4 with 33 3/8" arms
Quick out of stance — foot speed, balance, redirect quickness
Can stay mirrored on an island; keen feel in pass pro
First-team All-Big 12
weaknesses: Young / developmental — redshirt sophomore, only 24 career starts
Still filling out frame; physical development incomplete
Technique raw in run game — needs NFL coaching
Not NFL-ready-strong yet at POA
caleb lomu.nfl_comp: Developmental LT with high pass-pro ceiling

peter woods.play_style: Clemson DT drafted #29 by Chiefs. 6-3/298. AP All-American as junior (first Clemson DT since Wilkins). 4.75 forty — remarkable DT speed. Still filling frame.
strengths: Rare DT speed — 4.75 forty with quick first step
Size/athleticism combo — beats blockers with bull rush
Chases down ball carriers in run game; fights out of double teams
AP All-American at DT — high-level performance on loaded Clemson DL
weaknesses: Needs to add play strength as he fills out frame
Not a pure 1-tech or 3-tech — scheme-fit uncertain
Inconsistent — faced constant double/triple teams in 2025
Counting stats modest; flashes > sustained dominance

omar cooper.play_style: Indiana WR drafted #30 by Jets (trade-up). 6-0/202 physical possession receiver. 4.42 forty. Strong hands (5 drops in 3 seasons), fearless over middle, YAC monster.
strengths: One of the most physical WRs in class — tough tackle after catch
Elite hands — only 5 drops in 3 college seasons
Fearless over the middle; makes collision catches
4.42 forty — better than film speed; eased athletic concerns
Played boundary AND slot at high level — NFL chess-piece value
Willing blocker; competitive demeanor
weaknesses: Route tree limited — Indiana's RPO scheme hid full route diversity
Catch-point toughness vs elite NFL corners unproven
Run blocking needs polish despite willingness
Good but not elite long speed — not a pure burner

keldric faulk.play_style: Auburn DL drafted #31 by Titans. 6-6/275 with NFL-ready body at age 20. Strong-side power edge — best as a run defender first, pass rush still developing.
strengths: Great size/length — NFL-ready 6-6/275 frame at age 20
Elite POA strength — 700 squat / 415 bench; gets extension on blocks
NFL-caliber run defense — 62 run stops lead all FBS edges over 3 years
High-motor rusher — chases cleanup sacks
Sheds blocks well in run game
weaknesses: Hand usage as pass rusher raw — lets OTs land first contact
Gets chest-to-chest; doesn't work tackles' hands or counter
Subpar bend — some lower-body stiffness to turn corner
Relies on power without Plan B — NFL anchors will control him
Coverage sack profile more than primary creator early

jadarian price.play_style: Notre Dame RB drafted #32 by Seahawks. 5-11/210 with elite speed/explosiveness. Played behind Love — only 6.2 YPC in 2025, zero career starts. Scheme-versatile, vision-first.
strengths: Natural, instinctive patient runner with elite vision
Scheme-versatile — zone and gap concepts equally comfortable
Explosive speed — one of fastest/most explosive backs in class
6.2 YPC in 2025 — efficient when given carries
Contributes in pass game; combines vision + acceleration + physicality
Between-the-tackles physical runner despite speed profile
weaknesses: Zero career starts — backup role behind Love limited reps
Early-career injury recovery — durability question
Less contact balance than top RB prospects in class
Production profile thinner — depth-chart reality at ND
jadarian price.nfl_comp: Speed-and-vision rotational RB with starter upside
